questions.question: Explain the lossless-join property of decomposition with an example.
keyPoints: Definition: A decomposition is lossless if natural join of decomposed relations gives exactly the original relation
Condition: (R1 ∩ R2) → R1 or (R1 ∩ R2) → R2 must hold
Example with relation and FD showing the test

questions.question: What are update, insertion, and deletion anomalies? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Update anomaly: updating one instance doesn't update all duplicates
Insertion anomaly: cannot insert certain data without unrelated data
Deletion anomaly: deleting one fact removes unrelated information
Example showing each anomaly type

questions.question: Differentiate between BCNF and 3NF with respect to decomposition.
keyPoints: BCNF: every determinant must be superkey
3NF: determinant is superkey OR result is prime attribute
BCNF may not preserve dependencies, 3NF always preserves
Trade-off between normalization and practical enforcement

questions.question: What is dependency-preserving decomposition? Why is it desirable?
keyPoints: Definition: closure of projected FDs equals original FD closure
All original constraints can be enforced without joins
Desirable: maintains data integrity, simplifies constraint checking
Without it, joins required to enforce some FDs

questions.question: Explain the canonical cover of functional dependencies and its importance in decomposition.
keyPoints: Canonical cover is minimal equivalent set of FDs
No extraneous attributes in left or right side
No redundant FDs
Used to simplify decomposition process and ensure minimal relations

questions.question: Describe the algorithm for BCNF decomposition.
keyPoints: Identify FD violating BCNF (X → Y where X is not superkey)
Decompose relation into R1(X∪Y) and R2(R - (Y - X))
Repeat until all relations in BCNF
May not preserve all dependencies

questions.question: Given relation R(A,B,C,D) with FDs A→B, B→C, C→D, perform 3NF decomposition.
keyPoints: Find canonical cover: A→B, B→C, C→D
Identify candidate key: A (since A→B→C→D)
Relations from canonical cover: R1(A,B), R2(B,C), R3(C,D)
Add relation for candidate key if needed: R4(A) or check if A in R1
